revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''滑川のネブタ流し'''（なめりかわのねぶたながし）は、毎年7月31日に[[富山県]][[滑川市]]中川原海岸（通称 和田の浜）...'
revision.text:
'''滑川のネブタ流し'''（なめりかわのねぶたながし）は、毎年7月31日に[[富山県]][[滑川市]]中川原海岸（通称 和田の浜）で行われる、燃え盛る[[たいまつ]]を海にながし、[[厄除け]]、無病息災の祈願、また睡魔を払う[[火祭り]]である。ネブタ流しとして[[日本海側]]での南限とされ、[[1999年]]（[[平成]]11年）12月21日に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 概要 == 木枠の台座に立てたたいまつは高さ約4～6m太さは根元が0.7～0.8m、先端が0.3～0.4m、[[青竹]]に[[藁]]、[[むしろ]]など巻いて荒縄で縛ったもので、息を吹きかけ災いを移した人形（ひとがた）として、[[なす]]や[[きゅうり]]に目鼻をつけ紙製の服を着せたもの、人形に切った紙、願い事を書いた[[短冊]]などを飾り物としてたいまつに刺してある。
午後2時ごろ[[禊]]場（みそぎば）である櫟原（いちはら）神社で[[茅の輪くぐり|大祓]]の[[神事]]を行ったあと、夕方の5時ごろ和田の浜に向け住民や子供たちが担いで’’’「ネブタ流され、朝起きゃれ（起きれ）」’’’と掛け声を掛けながら地区内をまわり、浜辺に到着した後午後6時半ごろに点火し、大人も子供もたいまつといっしょに海へ入り、大人達は最後まで綺麗に燃え尽きるよう手助けしながら見守る。
かつてこの和田の浜一帯の町内でネブタが流されていたが、現在では中川原、常磐町1区・2区・3区の保存団体4町内と吾妻町、滑川青年会議所、滑川市内の寺家小学校などが参加して行われており、[[2010年]]（[[平成]]22年）のネブタ流しには11基のネブタが流された。
なお、ねぶたは人々を悩ます眠気といわれ、7月31日以降昼寝をしてはいけないと伝えられている。
== 外部リンク == [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm 滑川のネブタ流し] [http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/ 国指定文化財等データベース]
== 参考文献 == とやま祭ガイド（[[北日本新聞社]]）[[2004年]]（平成16年）3月31日発行
== 関連項目 == ねぶた
{{Event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:なめりかわのねふたなかし}} [[Category:富山県の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:滑川市]]

revision.comment: ちょこちょこと弄る
revision.text:
'''滑川のネブタ流し'''（なめりかわのねぶたながし）は、毎年7月31日に[[富山県]][[滑川市]]中川原海岸（通称 和田の浜）で行われる、燃え盛る[[たいまつ]]を海にながし、[[厄除け]]、無病息災の祈願、また睡魔を払う[[火祭り]]である。[[ねぶた]]としては[[日本海側]]での南限とされ、[[平成]]11年（[[1999年]]）12月21日に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 概要 == 木枠の台座に立てたたいまつは高さ約4から6[[メートル]]、太さは根元が0.7から0.8メートル、先端が0.3から0.4メートル、[[青竹]]に[[藁]]、[[むしろ]]などを巻いて荒縄で縛ったもので、息を吹きかけ災いを移した[[形代|人形（ひとがた）]]として、[[茄子]]や[[胡瓜]]に目鼻をつけ紙製の服を着せたもの、人形に切った紙、願い事を書いた[[短冊]]などを飾り物としてたいまつに刺してある。
午後2時ごろ[[禊]]場（みそぎば）である[[櫟原神社|櫟原（いちはら）神社]]で[[茅の輪くぐり|大祓]]の[[神事]]を行ったあと、夕方の5時ごろ和田の浜に向け住民や子供たちが担いで「ネブタ流され、朝起きゃれ（起きれ）」と掛け声を掛けながら地区内をまわり、浜辺に到着した後の午後6時半ごろに点火し、大人も子供もたいまつといっしょに海へ入り、大人達は最後まで綺麗に燃え尽きるよう手助けしながら見守る。
かつてこの和田の浜一帯の町内でネブタが流されていたが、現在では中川原、常磐町1区・2区・3区の保存団体4町内と吾妻町、滑川青年会議所、滑川市内の寺家小学校などが参加して行われており、平成22年（[[2010年]]）のネブタ流しには11基のネブタが流された。
なお、「ねぶた」は人々を悩ます眠気といわれ、7月31日以降昼寝をしてはいけないと伝えられている。
== 参考文献 == *『とやま祭ガイド』[[北日本新聞社]]、2004年（平成16年）
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm 滑川のネブタ流し]（滑川市観光協会） *[http://www.bunka.go.jp/bsys/maindetails.asp?register_id=302&item_id=715 滑川のネブタ流し]（国指定文化財等データベース）
{{Event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:なめりかわのねふたなかし}} [[Category:富山県の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:滑川市]]

'''滑川のネブタ流し'''（なめりかわのねぶたながし）は、毎年7月31日に[[富山県]][[滑川市]]中川原海岸（通称 和田の浜）で行われる、燃え盛る[[たいまつ]]を海にながし、[[厄除け]]、無病息災の祈願、また睡魔を払う[[火祭り]]である。[[ねぶた]]としては[[日本海側]]での南限とされ、[[平成]]11年（[[1999年]]）12月21日に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 概要 == 木枠の台座に立てたたいまつは高さ約4から6[[メートル]]、太さは根元が0.7から0.8メートル、先端が0.3から0.4メートル、[[青竹]]に[[藁]]、[[むしろ]]などを巻いて荒縄で縛ったもので、息を吹きかけ災いを移した[[形代|人形（ひとがた）]]として、[[茄子]]や[[胡瓜]]に目鼻をつけ紙製の服を着せたもの、人形に切った紙、願い事を書いた[[短冊]]などを飾り物としてたいまつに刺してある。
午後2時ごろ[[禊]]場（みそぎば）である[[櫟原神社 (滑川市神明町)|櫟原（いちはら）神社]]で[[茅の輪くぐり|大祓]]の[[神事]]を行ったあと、夕方の5時ごろ和田の浜に向け住民や子供たちが担いで「ネブタ流され、朝起きゃれ（起きれ）」と掛け声を掛けながら地区内をまわり、浜辺に到着した後の午後6時半ごろに点火し、大人も子供もたいまつといっしょに海へ入り、大人達は最後まで綺麗に燃え尽きるよう手助けしながら見守る。
かつてこの和田の浜一帯の町内でネブタが流されていたが、現在では中川原、常磐町1区・2区・3区の保存団体4町内と吾妻町、滑川青年会議所、滑川市内の[[滑川市立寺家小学校|寺家小学校]]などの参加で行われており、平成22年（[[2010年]]）のネブタ流しには11基のネブタが、平成24年（[[2012年]]）には初参加の滑川東地区公民館が加わり）<ref>北日本新聞 2012年8月1日45面</ref>12基のネブタが流された。
なお、「ねぶた」は人々を悩ます眠気といわれ、7月31日以降昼寝をしてはいけないと伝えられている。
== 参考文献 == *『とやま祭ガイド』（[[北日本新聞社]]）平成16年（[[2004年]]）3月31日発行 ISBN4-906678-87-4
== 脚註 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[滑川市]] *[[ねぶた]] *[[重要無形民俗文化財]]
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm 滑川のネブタ流し]（滑川市観光協会） *{{国指定文化財等データベース|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}}
{{Event-stub}}

=== 現在もネブタ流しを行う町及び団体 === * 中川原、常磐町1区、常磐町2区、常磐町3区、吾妻町、滑川青年会議所、[[滑川市立寺家小学校]]、滑川東地区公民館
=== 以前はネブタ流しを行っていた町 === * 神明町、寺家町、三穂町、神家町、瀬羽町、田中町、加島町2区（旧 西町）、加島町3区（旧 堺町）、領家町、高月町東部、高月町西部

revision.comment: /* ネブタを流す町 */
revision.text:
'''滑川のネブタ流し'''（なめりかわのねぶたながし）は、毎年7月31日に[[富山県]][[滑川市]]中川原海岸（通称 和田の浜）で行われる、燃え盛る[[たいまつ]]を海にながし、[[厄除け]]、無病息災の祈願、また睡魔を払う[[火祭り]]である。[[ねぶた]]としては[[日本海側]]での南限とされ、[[1999年]]（[[平成]]11年）12月21日に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。また[[2006年]]（平成18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
== 概要 == 木枠の台座に立てたたいまつは高さ約4から6[[メートル]]、太さは根元が0.7から0.8メートル、先端が0.3から0.4メートル、[[青竹]]に[[藁]]、[[むしろ]]などを巻いて荒縄で縛ったもので、息を吹きかけ災いを移した[[形代|人形（ひとがた）]]として、[[茄子]]や[[胡瓜]]に目鼻をつけ紙製の服を着せたもの、人形に切った紙、願い事を書いた[[短冊]]などを飾り物としてたいまつに刺してある。
午後2時ごろ[[禊]]場（みそぎば）である[[櫟原神社 (滑川市神明町)|櫟原（いちはら）神社]]で[[茅の輪くぐり|大祓]]の[[神事]]を行ったあと、夕方の5時ごろ和田の浜に向け住民や子供たちが担いで「ネブタ流され、朝起きゃれ（起きれ）」と掛け声を掛けながら地区内をまわり、浜辺に到着した後の午後6時半ごろに点火し、大人も子供もたいまつといっしょに海へ入り、大人達は最後まで綺麗に燃え尽きるよう手助けしながら見守る。なお、「ねぶた」は人々を悩ます眠気といわれ、7月31日以降昼寝をしてはいけないと伝えられている。
== ネブタを流す町 == かつて和田の浜一帯の町内でネブタが流されていたが、現在では中川原、常磐町1区・2区・3区の保存団体4町内と吾妻町、滑川[[青年会議所]]、滑川市内の[[滑川市立寺家小学校|寺家小学校]]などの参加で行われており、[[2010年]]（平成22年）のネブタ流しには11基のネブタが、[[2012年]]（平成24年）には初参加の滑川東地区公民館が加わり<ref>[[北日本新聞]] 2012年8月1日45面</ref>計12基のネブタが流された。
=== 現在もネブタ流しを行う町及び団体 === * 中川原、常磐町1区、常磐町2区、常磐町3区、吾妻町、滑川青年会議所、[[滑川市立寺家小学校]]、滑川東地区公民館
=== 以前はネブタ流しを行っていた町 === * 神明町、寺家町、三穂町、神家町、瀬羽町、田中町、加島町2区（旧 西町）、加島町3区（旧 堺町）、領家町、高月町東部、高月町西部
== 参考文献 == *『滑川のネブタ流しと 夏を彩る民俗行事』（[[滑川市立博物館]] 編・滑川市[[教育委員会]]）[[1999年]]（平成11年）3月31日発行 *『とやま祭ガイド』（[[北日本新聞社]]）[[2004年]]（平成16年）3月31日発行 ISBN 4-906678-87-4 *『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2007年]]（平成19年）3月発行
== 脚註 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[滑川市]] *[[ねぶた]] *[[重要無形民俗文化財]] *[[日本の祭一覧]]
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm 滑川のネブタ流し]（滑川市観光協会） *{{国指定文化財等データベース|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}}
{{Event-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:なめりかわのねふたなかし}} [[Category:富山県の祭り]] [[Category:重要無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:滑川市]] [[Category:ねぶた]]

== ネブタを流す町 == かつて和田の浜一帯の町内でネブタが流されていたが、現在では中川原、常磐町1区・2区・3区の保存団体4町内と吾妻町、滑川[[青年会議所]]、滑川市内の[[滑川市立寺家小学校|寺家小学校]]などの参加で行われており、[[2010年]]（平成22年）のネブタ流しには11基のネブタが、[[2012年]]（平成24年）には初参加の滑川東地区[[公民館]]が加わり<ref>[[北日本新聞]] 2012年8月1日45面</ref>計12基のネブタが流された。
=== 現在もネブタ流しを行う町及び団体 === * 中川原、常磐町1区、常磐町2区、常磐町3区、吾妻町、滑川青年会議所、[[滑川市立寺家小学校]]、滑川東地区公民館
=== 以前はネブタ流しを行っていた町 === * 神明町、寺家町、三穂町、神家町、瀬羽町、田中町、加島町2区（旧 堺町）、加島町3区（旧 西町）、領家町、高月町東部、高月町西部

{{画像提供依頼|date=2017年4月17日|cat=滑川市|祭風景}} '''滑川のネブタ流し'''（なめりかわのねぶたながし）は、毎年7月31日に[[富山県]][[滑川市]]中川原海岸（通称 和田の浜）で行われる、燃え盛る[[たいまつ]]を海にながし、[[厄除け]]、無病息災の祈願、また睡魔を払う[[火祭り]]である。[[ねぶた]]としては[[日本海側]]での南限とされ、[[1999年]]（[[平成]]11年）12月21日に国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。また[[2006年]]（平成18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。

[[1999年]]（[[平成]]11年）12月21日に国の重要無形民俗文化財に指定された。また[[2006年]]（平成18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
== ネブタを流す町 == かつて和田の浜一帯の町内でネブタが流されていたが、現在では中川原、常磐町1区・2区・3区の保存団体4町内と吾妻町、滑川[[青年会議所]]、滑川市内の[[滑川市立寺家小学校|寺家小学校]]などの参加で行われており、[[2010年]]（平成22年）のネブタ流しには11基のネブタが、[[2012年]]（平成24年）には初参加の滑川東地区[[公民館]]が加わり計12基<ref>[[北日本新聞]] 2012年8月1日45面</ref>、[[2017年]]（平成29年）には滑川[[市役所]]が参加し計11基のネブタが流された<ref>『祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し』北日本新聞 2017年8月1日43面</ref>。
=== 現在もネブタ流しを行う町及び団体 === * 中川原、常磐町1区、常磐町2区、常磐町3区、吾妻町、滑川青年会議所、[[滑川市立寺家小学校]]、滑川東地区公民館、滑川市役所など
=== 以前はネブタ流しを行っていた町 === * 神明町、寺家町、三穂町、神家町、瀬羽町、田中町、加島町2区（旧 堺町）、加島町3区（旧 西町）、領家町、高月町東部、高月町西部
== 脚註 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『滑川のネブタ流しと 夏を彩る民俗行事』（[[滑川市立博物館]] 編・滑川市[[教育委員会]]）[[1999年]]（平成11年）3月31日発行 * 『とやま祭ガイド』（[[北日本新聞社]]）[[2004年]]（平成16年）3月31日発行 ISBN 4-906678-87-4 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2007年]]（平成19年）3月発行
== 関連項目 == * [[滑川市]] * [[ねぶた]] * [[火祭り]] * [[重要無形民俗文化財]] * [[日本の祭一覧]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm 滑川のネブタ流し]（滑川市観光協会） * {{国指定文化財等データベース|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}} * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf#search='%E5%AF%8C%E5%B1%B1%E3%81%AE%E7%A5%AD%E3%82%8A' 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）]
{{Event-stub}}

{{画像提供依頼|date=2017年4月17日|cat=滑川市|祭風景}} '''滑川のネブタ流し'''（なめりかわのねぶたながし）は、毎年7月31日に[[富山県]][[滑川市]]中川原海岸（通称 和田の浜）で行われる、燃え盛る[[たいまつ]]を海にながし、[[厄除け]]、無病息災の祈願、また睡魔を払う[[火祭り]]であり、[[ねぶた]]としては[[日本海側]]での南限とされ、国の[[重要無形民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 概要 == 木枠の台座に立てたたいまつは高さ約4から6[[メートル]]、太さは根元が0.7から0.8メートル、先端が0.3から0.4メートル、[[青竹]]に[[藁]]、[[むしろ]]などを巻いて荒縄で縛ったもので、息を吹きかけ災いを移した[[形代|人形（ひとがた）]]として、[[茄子]]や[[胡瓜]]に目鼻をつけ紙製の服を着せたもの、人形に切った紙、願い事を書いた[[短冊]]などを飾り物としてたいまつに刺してある。
午後2時ごろ[[禊]]場（みそぎば）である[[櫟原神社 (滑川市神明町)|櫟原（いちはら）神社]]で[[茅の輪くぐり|大祓]]の[[神事]]を行ったあと、夕方の5時ごろ和田の浜（[[ほたるいかミュージアム]]裏手）に向け住民や子供たちが担いで「ネブタ流され、朝起きゃれ（起きれ）」と掛け声を掛けながら地区内をまわり、浜辺に到着した後の午後6時半ごろに点火し、大人も子供もたいまつといっしょに海へ入り、大人達は最後まで綺麗に燃え尽きるよう手助けしながら見守る。なお、「ねぶた」は人々を悩ます眠気といわれ、7月31日以降昼寝をしてはいけないと伝えられている。
[[1999年]]（[[平成]]11年）12月21日に国の重要無形民俗文化財に指定された。また[[2006年]]（平成18年）には、「とやまの文化財百選（とやまの祭り百選部門）」に選定されている。
== ネブタを流す町 == かつて和田の浜一帯の町内でネブタが流されていたが、現在では中川原、常磐町1区・2区・3区の保存団体4町内と吾妻町、滑川[[青年会議所]]、滑川市内の[[滑川市立寺家小学校|寺家小学校]]などの参加で行われており、[[2010年]]（平成22年）のネブタ流しには11基のネブタが、[[2012年]]（平成24年）には初参加の滑川東地区[[公民館]]が加わり計12基<ref>[[北日本新聞]] 2012年8月1日45面</ref>、[[2017年]]（平成29年）には滑川[[市役所]]が参加し計11基のネブタが流された<ref>『祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し』北日本新聞 2017年8月1日43面</ref>。[[2018年]]（平成30年）には滑川西地区公民館が初参加した<ref>『祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し』北日本新聞 2018年8月1日42面</ref>。
=== 現在もネブタ流しを行う町及び団体 === * 中川原、常磐町1区、常磐町2区、常磐町3区、吾妻町、滑川青年会議所、[[滑川市立寺家小学校]]、滑川東地区公民館、滑川西地区公民館、滑川市役所など
=== 以前はネブタ流しを行っていた町 === * 神明町、寺家町、三穂町、神家町、瀬羽町、田中町、加島町2区（旧 堺町）、加島町3区（旧 西町）、領家町、高月町東部、高月町西部
== 脚註 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『滑川のネブタ流しと 夏を彩る民俗行事』（[[滑川市立博物館]] 編・滑川市[[教育委員会]]）[[1999年]]（平成11年）3月31日発行 * 『とやま祭ガイド』（[[北日本新聞社]]）[[2004年]]（平成16年）3月31日発行 ISBN 4-906678-87-4 * 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）[[2007年]]（平成19年）3月発行
== 関連項目 == * [[滑川市]] * [[ねぶた]] * [[火祭り]] * [[重要無形民俗文化財]] * [[日本の祭一覧]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm 滑川のネブタ流し]（滑川市観光協会） * {{国指定文化財等データベース|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}} * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf#search='%E5%AF%8C%E5%B1%B1%E3%81%AE%E7%A5%AD%E3%82%8A' 『とやまの文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り』（富山県教育委員会 生涯学習・文化財室）]
{{Event-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a {{ill|fire festival|ja|火祭り}} held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in {{ill|Namerikawa, Toyama|ja|滑川市}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県}}. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with {{ill|straw|ja|藁}}, {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ({{ill|hitogata|ja|形代}}), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ({{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓}}) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
<!--Nebuta Nagashi-->[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=Namerikawa Municipal Museum|title=滑川のネブタ流しと夏を彩る民俗行事|publisher=Namerikawa City Board of Education|year=1999}} * {{Cite book|editor=Hokuriku Shinbunsha|title=とやま祭りガイド|publisher=Hokuriku Shinbunsha|year=2004|ISBN=4-906678-87-4}} * {{Cite book|editor=Toyama Prefecture Board of Education|title=とやまの祭り|publisher=Toyama Prefecture Board of Education|year=2007}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧}}
== External Links == * [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi Official Site] * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}} * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf 富山の文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り]
{{Event-stub}}

{{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a {{ill|fire festival|ja|火祭り|12=draft}} held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in {{ill|Namerikawa, Toyama|ja|滑川市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}}. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|12=draft}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|12=draft}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with {{ill|straw|ja|藁|12=draft}}, {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=draft}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ({{ill|hitogata|ja|形代|12=draft}}), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=draft}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=draft}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=draft}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ({{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓|12=draft}}) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=draft}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
<!--Nebuta Nagashi-->[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=draft}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=draft}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West

For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:qq]]" to ""
revision.text:
{{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a {{ill|fire festival|ja|火祭り|12=draft}} held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in {{ill|Namerikawa, Toyama|ja|滑川市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|ja|富山県|12=draft}}. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|12=draft}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|12=draft}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with {{ill|straw|ja|藁|12=draft}}, {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=draft}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ({{ill|hitogata|ja|形代|12=draft}}), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=draft}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=draft}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=draft}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ({{ill|Ōharae|ja|大祓|12=draft}}) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=draft}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
<!--Nebuta Nagashi-->[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=draft}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=draft}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|editor=Namerikawa Municipal Museum|title=滑川のネブタ流しと夏を彩る民俗行事|publisher=Namerikawa City Board of Education|year=1999}} * {{Cite book|editor=Hokuriku Shinbunsha|title=とやま祭りガイド|publisher=Hokuriku Shinbunsha|year=2004|ISBN=4-906678-87-4}} * {{Cite book|editor=Toyama Prefecture Board of Education|title=とやまの祭り|publisher=Toyama Prefecture Board of Education|year=2007}} == See Also == * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|ja|日本の祭一覧|12=draft}}
== External Links == * [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi Official Site] * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}} * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf 富山の文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り]

'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a [[:en:Festival of fire|Fire festival]] held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in [[:en:Namerikawa, Toyama|Namerikawa, Toyama]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torches|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいまつ|1=torch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327954}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nebuta|qq=|1=nebuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q861941}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with [[:en:Straw|straw]], {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ([[:en:Katashiro|hitogata]]), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Solanum melongena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumis sativus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|qq=|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ([[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]]) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|qq=|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|qq=|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|qq=|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. <!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--> [[Category:Nebuta]] [[Category:Festivals in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Namerikawa, Toyama]] [[Category:Image requests in Namerikawa City]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11565005}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|qq=|" to "|"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11565005]] Wikidata name: Namerikawa Nebuta Nagashi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a [[:en:Festival of fire|Fire festival]] held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in [[:en:Namerikawa, Toyama|Namerikawa, Toyama]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいまつ|1=torch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327954}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nebuta|1=nebuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q861941}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with [[:en:Straw|straw]], {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ([[:en:Katashiro|hitogata]]), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Solanum melongena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumis sativus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ([[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]]) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West

== External Links == * [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi Official Site] * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}} * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf 富山の文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り]

At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11565005]] Wikidata name: Namerikawa Nebuta Nagashi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a [[:en:Festival of fire|Fire festival]] held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in [[:en:Namerikawa, Toyama|Namerikawa, Toyama]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいまつ|1=torch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327954}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nebuta|1=nebuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q861941}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with [[:en:Straw|straw]], {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ([[:en:Katashiro|hitogata]]), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Solanum melongena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumis sativus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ([[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]]) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>

<!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--><!--Nebuta Nagashi--> [[Category:Nebuta]] [[Category:Festivals in Toyama Prefecture]] [[Category:Namerikawa, Toyama]] [[Category:Image requests in Namerikawa City]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11565005}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11565005]] Wikidata name: Namerikawa Nebuta Nagashi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a [[:en:Festival of fire|Fire festival]] held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in [[:en:Namerikawa, Toyama|Namerikawa, Toyama]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいまつ|1=torch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327954}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nebuta|1=nebuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q861941}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with [[:en:Straw|straw]], {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ([[:en:Katashiro|hitogata]]), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Solanum melongena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumis sativus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ([[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]]) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West

== External Links == * [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi Official Site] * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}} * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf 富山の文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り]

'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a [[:en:Festival of fire|Fire festival]] held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in [[:en:Namerikawa, Toyama|Namerikawa, Toyama]], [[:en:Toyama Prefecture|Toyama Prefecture]]. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいまつ|1=torch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327954}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nebuta|1=nebuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q861941}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with [[:en:Straw|straw]], {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ([[:en:Katashiro|hitogata]]), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Solanum melongena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumis sativus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ([[:en:Ōharae-shiki|Ōharae]]) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West

The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>

=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11565005]] Wikidata name: Namerikawa Nebuta Nagashi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a {{ill|Festival of fire|en|Festival of fire|WD=Q3135724|lt=Fire festival}} held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in {{ill|Namerikawa, Toyama|en|Namerikawa, Toyama|WD=Q823513|lt=Namerikawa, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいまつ|1=torch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327954}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nebuta|1=nebuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q861941}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with {{ill|Straw|en|Straw|WD=Q160066|lt=straw}}, {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ({{ill|Katashiro|en|Katashiro|WD=Q11487867|lt=hitogata}}), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Solanum melongena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumis sativus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ({{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|Ōharae-shiki|WD=Q11261910|lt=Ōharae}}) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:滑川のネブタ流し]]
{{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a {{ill|Festival of fire|en|Festival of fire|WD=Q3135724|lt=Fire festival}} held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in {{ill|Namerikawa, Toyama|en|Namerikawa, Toyama|WD=Q823513|lt=Namerikawa, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいまつ|1=torch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327954}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nebuta|1=nebuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q861941}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with {{ill|Straw|en|Straw|WD=Q160066|lt=straw}}, {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ({{ill|Katashiro|en|Katashiro|WD=Q11487867|lt=hitogata}}), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Solanum melongena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumis sativus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ({{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|Ōharae-shiki|WD=Q11261910|lt=Ōharae}}) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West

== External Links == * [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi Official Site] * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}} * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf 富山の文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り]

{{Image requested|date=April 17, 2017|cat=Namerikawa City|Festival scenes}}
'''Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi''' (滑川のネブタ流し) is a {{ill|Festival of fire|en|Festival of fire|WD=Q3135724|lt=Fire festival}} held annually on July 31st at Nakagawara Beach (locally known as Wada no Hama) in {{ill|Namerikawa, Toyama|en|Namerikawa, Toyama|WD=Q823513|lt=Namerikawa, Toyama}}, {{ill|Toyama Prefecture|en|Toyama Prefecture|WD=Q132929|lt=Toyama Prefecture}}. It involves floating blazing {{ill|torches|ja|松明|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torches|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to たいまつ|1=torch|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q327954}} into the sea to pray for protection from misfortune, good health, and to ward off sleepiness. It is considered the southernmost occurrence of the {{ill|nebuta|ja|ねぶた|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nebuta|1=nebuta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q861941}} festival along the Sea of Japan and has been designated as an {{ill|Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|重要無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property|1=Important Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q6573893}} of Japan.
== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with {{ill|Straw|en|Straw|WD=Q160066|lt=straw}}, {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ({{ill|Katashiro|en|Katashiro|WD=Q11487867|lt=hitogata}}), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Solanum melongena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumis sativus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ({{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|Ōharae-shiki|WD=Q11261910|lt=Ōharae}}) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West
== References == {{Reflist}}

== External Links == * [http://www.namerikawashikankou.com/nebuta2010.htm Namerikawa no Nebuta Nagashi Official Site] * {{Cultural Properties of Japan Database|302|715|滑川のネブタ流し}} * [http://www.pref.toyama.jp/sections/3009/3007/digital/03-event/maturi/maturi.pdf 富山の文化財百選シリーズ(3) とやまの祭り]

== Overview == The torches are constructed on wooden bases, standing approximately 4 to 6 meters tall, with a base diameter of 0.7 to 0.8 meters and a top diameter of 0.3 to 0.4 meters. The torches are wrapped with {{ill|Straw|en|Straw|WD=Q160066|lt=straw|1=Straw}}, {{ill|mats|ja|むしろ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mats|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}}, and bound with rough ropes. Decorative elements include effigies ({{ill|Katashiro|en|Katashiro|WD=Q11487867|lt=hitogata|1=Katashiro}}), made from items such as {{ill|eggplants|ja|茄子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/eggplants|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ナス|1=Eggplant|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7540}} or {{ill|cucumbers|ja|胡瓜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cucumbers|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キュウリ|1=Cucumber|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23425}} adorned with facial features, dressed in paper clothing, paper cutouts, or {{ill|tanzaku|ja|短冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tanzaku|1=tanzaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3515381}} inscribed with prayers.
Around 2:00 PM, a purification ritual ({{ill|Ōharae-shiki|en|Ōharae-shiki|WD=Q11261910|lt=Ōharae|1=Ōharae-shiki}}) is performed at the {{ill|Ichihara Shrine|ja|櫟原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichihara Shrine|1=Ichihara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q123115222}} purification site. At approximately 5:00 PM, participants carry the torches to Wada no Hama, chanting "Nebuta nagasare, asa okyare" (meaning "Drive away the nebuta and wake up in the morning"). After arriving at the beach, the torches are ignited at around 6:30 PM. Both children and adults enter the sea with the torches, ensuring they burn out completely. It is said that "nebuta" symbolizes sleepiness, and from July 31st onward, daytime napping is forbidden according to tradition.
The festival was designated as a national Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property on December 21, 1999, and in 2006 it was selected as one of the "100 Cultural Properties of Toyama" (Festivals Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]]
[[Category) by the Toyama Prefecture Board of Education.
== Towns Participating in Nebuta Nagashi == Historically, towns along Wada no Hama participated in the festival. Today, it is conducted by four preservation groups from Nakagawara and Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), along with contributions from Azumamachi, the {{ill|Namerikawa Junior Chamber|ja|日本青年会議所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Junior Chamber|1=Junior Chamber International Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509400}}, {{ill|Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|ja|滑川市立寺家小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namerikawa Municipal Jike Elementary School|1=Namerikawa City Jike Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565015}}, and others. For example: - In 2010, 11 nebuta floats were launched. - In 2012, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center joined, increasing the count to 12.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2012-08-01}}</ref> - In 2017, Namerikawa City Hall joined, and 11 floats were launched.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 赤々と 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2017-08-01}}</ref> - In 2018, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center participated for the first time.<ref>{{Cite news|title=祈りの炎 海染める 滑川ネブタ流し|newspaper=北日本新聞|date=2018-08-01}}</ref>
=== Current Participants === - Nakagawara, Tokiwamachi (1st, 2nd, and 3rd Districts), Azumamachi, Namerikawa Junior Chamber, Jike Elementary School, Namerikawa Higashi District Community Center, Namerikawa Nishi District Community Center, Namerikawa City Hall
=== Former Participants === - Shinmeicho, Jikemachi, Mihomachi, Kamiyamachi, Sawamachi, Tanakamachi, Kajimamachi (2nd and 3rd Districts), Ryokemachi, Takatsukimachi East, Takatsukimachi West
